ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302114 covers shrimp ball as a general category; mustard flavoring variant is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers processed shrimps and prawns as a category. No specific code exists for mustard-flavored shrimp balls; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for mustard-flavored shrimp balls as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of mustard flavored shrimp balls consists of approximately 5 to 6 pieces, weighing around 150 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp (prawn) ball, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; adapted with mustard seasoning adjustment
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for mustard-flavored shrimp balls exists in approved primary sources. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for fried shrimp/prawn balls (NDB 15150 and related entries) and FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for processed shrimp products. Mustard contributes negligible macronutrient change at typical seasoning quantities. Sodium is elevated slightly to reflect mustard and seasoning additions. Values represent a best-estimate composite for a fried shrimp ball product at approximately 60–65% shrimp paste, 15% starch binder, 5% egg white, and seasoning.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed seafood / fish ball products — analogous estimate based on fried fish ball GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mustard-flavored shrimp balls. GI estimated at 40 based on analogy with fried fish balls and processed seafood products, which are high-protein, moderate-starch foods with low-to-moderate GI values (range 35–50 in peer-reviewed literature). Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as (40 × 10.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 6.1. gi_category set to 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~780 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 pieces and avoid additional dipping sauces high in sodium, as a full serving can contribute approximately 33% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content (~10 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mustard flavored shrimp balls have a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 40 and a glycemic load of approximately 6 per serving, making them a relatively suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions without high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g), elevated sodium, and cholesterol (~115 mg per 100 g) from deep-frying and shrimp content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption due to the combined effect of elevated sodium, dietary cholesterol from shrimp, and saturated fat from deep-frying; choosing steamed or pan-fried preparations and limiting portion to 3–4 pieces per meal is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mustard Flavored Shrimp Balls (芥味虾球, Jiè wèi xiā qiú) are a Chinese-style processed seafood dish consisting of minced shrimp paste seasoned with mustard, white pepper, sesame oil, salt, and bound with starch and egg white, then shaped into round balls and deep-fried or pan-fried until golden. The dish is commonly served in Chinese restaurants in Singapore as a starter or side dish. The texture is firm and bouncy on the outside with a tender, juicy interior, and the mustard seasoning imparts a mild pungent heat that complements the natural sweetness of the shrimp. Nutritionally, shrimp balls are a good source of lean protein (~14.5 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (~9.8 g per 100 g) due to frying, and relatively low in carbohydrates (~10.2 g per 100 g) from the starch binder. The sodium content is elevated (~520 mg per 100 g) due to seasoning, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is low (~40), making this dish relatively suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. Shrimp are a natural source of selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and iodine. The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free but contains crustacean and egg allergens.

meal_context: Side dish in Chinese set meal

confidence_reason: No exact entry for mustard-flavored shrimp balls exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried shrimp/prawn ball data and FAO/INFOODS SEA processed seafood composition data. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature on similar fried seafood ball products. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source database for this food category. All other micronutrient values are adapted estimates from USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for fried processed shrimp products. Mustard flavoring at typical restaurant seasoning levels does not materially alter macronutrient composition. Sodium value is elevated relative to plain shrimp to account for mustard, salt, and seasoning additions typical of restaurant preparation. The price of SGD 26.80 from field survey data (Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant) reflects a full restaurant portion and is preserved from the input record for reference only; it is not used in nutritional calculations. GI estimated at 40 (low category, GI ≤ 55) based on analogy with fried fish and seafood ball products from peer-reviewed GI literature.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Adapted nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA for fried processed shrimp ball products. Added mustard seasoning sodium adjustment. Populated all mandatory multilingual fields (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Estimated GI at 40 from peer-reviewed analogous seafood ball data. Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value in notes. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 criteria satisfied on first iteration.
